name: mcp
context: Model Context Protocol — the open standard for connecting AI agents to tools, data sources, and prompt templates
info: The MCP ecosystem covers the Model Context Protocol, an open standard for connecting AI applications to external systems. MCP follows a client-server architecture where hosts (AI apps like Claude Desktop, VS Code, Cursor) create MCP clients that connect to MCP servers exposing tools, resources, and prompts. The protocol uses JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio or Streamable HTTP transports. MCP servers expose three core primitives: tools (executable functions the LLM can invoke), resources (contextual data like files and database schemas), and prompts (reusable interaction templates). Official SDKs exist for Python (mcp) and TypeScript (@modelcontextprotocol/sdk). MCP is supported by Claude, ChatGPT, VS Code Copilot, Cursor, and many other AI applications.
body:
# Model Context Protocol (MCP)

MCP is the open standard for connecting AI applications to external systems. Think of it as USB-C for AI — a universal interface that lets any agent talk to any tool server.

## Architecture

MCP follows a client-server model with three participants:

- **Host** — The AI application (Claude Desktop, VS Code, Cursor) that coordinates MCP clients
- **Client** — A component inside the host that maintains a 1:1 connection to an MCP server
- **Server** — A program that exposes tools, resources, and prompts to clients

```
Host (AI App)
  ├── MCP Client 1 ──── MCP Server A (local, stdio)
  ├── MCP Client 2 ──── MCP Server B (local, stdio)
  └── MCP Client 3 ──── MCP Server C (remote, HTTP)
```

## Core Primitives

MCP servers expose three types of capabilities:

| Primitive | Control | Description |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| **[[mcp/server/tools]]** | Model-controlled | Functions the LLM can invoke (search, compute, API calls) |
| **[[mcp/server/resources]]** | Application-controlled | Data sources providing context (files, DB schemas, API responses) |
| **[[mcp/server/prompts]]** | User-controlled | Reusable interaction templates (code review, analysis) |

## Protocol Layers

**Data Layer** — JSON-RPC 2.0 messages: lifecycle management, capability negotiation, primitive operations (`tools/list`, `tools/call`, `resources/read`, `prompts/get`).

**Transport Layer** — Communication channels: [[mcp/transport]] covers stdio (local subprocess) and Streamable HTTP (remote, with SSE streaming).

## SDKs

| SDK | Package | Install |
|-----|---------|----------|
| Python | `mcp` | `pip install "mcp[cli]"` |
| TypeScript | `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk` | `npm install @modelcontextprotocol/sdk` |

## Quick Start — Python Server

```python
from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP

mcp = FastMCP("my-server")

@mcp.tool()
def add(a: int, b: int) -> int:
    """Add two numbers."""
    return a + b

@mcp.resource("config://app")
def get_config() -> str:
    """Return application config."""
    return "App configuration data"

@mcp.prompt()
def review_code(code: str) -> str:
    """Create a code review prompt."""
    return f"Please review this code:\n\n{code}"

if __name__ == "__main__":
    mcp.run()
```

## Quick Start — TypeScript Server

```typescript
import { McpServer } from "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/server/mcp.js";
import { StdioServerTransport } from "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/server/stdio.js";
import { z } from "zod";

const server = new McpServer({ name: "my-server", version: "1.0.0" });

server.tool("add", { a: z.number(), b: z.number() }, async ({ a, b }) => ({
  content: [{ type: "text", text: String(a + b) }]
}));

const transport = new StdioServerTransport();
await server.connect(transport);
```

## Running an MCP Server

```bash
# Python — stdio transport
python my_server.py

# Python — with MCP CLI inspector
mcp dev my_server.py

# Python — install in Claude Desktop
mcp install my_server.py

# TypeScript — stdio transport
node my_server.js
```

## Configuration (Claude Desktop)

Add servers to `claude_desktop_config.json`:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "my-server": {
      "command": "python",
      "args": ["path/to/server.py"]
    },
    "remote-server": {
      "url": "https://example.com/mcp"
    }
  }
}
```

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|----------|
| `MCP_SERVER_NAME` | Server identity during initialization |
| `MCP_TRANSPORT` | Transport type: `stdio` or `http` |
| `MCP_PORT` | Port for HTTP transport (default: 3000) |

sections.title: Architecture overview
sections.body: MCP uses a host/client/server architecture. The host is the AI application (Claude Desktop, VS Code). It creates one MCP client per server connection. Each client maintains a dedicated JSON-RPC 2.0 session with its server. Local servers use stdio transport (subprocess). Remote servers use Streamable HTTP with optional SSE streaming. See [[mcp/architecture]] for the full breakdown.

sections.title: Core primitives
sections.body: Servers expose three primitive types: **Tools** are model-controlled functions the LLM invokes to perform actions. **Resources** are application-controlled data sources providing context. **Prompts** are user-controlled interaction templates. Each has list/get/call methods. See [[mcp/server/tools]], [[mcp/server/resources]], [[mcp/server/prompts]].

sections.title: Connection lifecycle
sections.body: MCP connections follow a lifecycle: 1) Client sends `initialize` with protocol version and capabilities, 2) Server responds with its capabilities (tools, resources, prompts), 3) Client sends `notifications/initialized`, 4) Normal message exchange begins, 5) Either party can close the connection. Capability negotiation determines what features are available.

sections.title: Official SDKs
sections.body: Python SDK: `pip install "mcp[cli]"` — provides `FastMCP` high-level API and `mcp dev` inspector. TypeScript SDK: `npm install @modelcontextprotocol/sdk` — provides `McpServer` class with Zod schema support. Both SDKs handle JSON-RPC framing, transport setup, and lifecycle management automatically.
